title: 23 Years: A Study of the Prophetic Career of Mohammad
description: 23 Years: A Study of the Prophetic Career of Mohammad is a biographical book by Ali Dashti. Considered banned and highly controversial in Iran due to critical examination of the life of Muhammad; led to the author's arrest and death sentence by revolutionary court.
descriptionParagraphs: 23 Years: A Study of the Prophetic Career of Mohammad is a biographical book by Ali Dashti. Considered banned and highly controversial in Iran due to critical examination of the life of Muhammad; led to the author's arrest and death sentence by revolutionary court.

What makes it interesting is that interpretation, devotion, satire, or doctrinal conflict becomes a matter of state administration. As a biographical book, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in Iran. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: Considered banned and highly controversial in Iran due to critical examination of the life of Muhammad; led to the author's arrest and death sentence by revolutionary court. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

banningParagraphs: 23 Years: A Study of the Prophetic Career of Mohammad entered censorship debates as a biographical book associated with doctrine, public controversy, and religion. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around religious control and religious offense.

The earliest event currently captured here is Date not yet pinned down in Iran, where Iran authorities banned publication or circulation. counterReadings.title: Assassins of the Mind
counterReadings.author: Christopher Hitchens
counterReadings.note: Frames the Rushdie affair as a test of free speech against violent religious intimidation.

counterReadings.title: From Fatwa to Jihad
counterReadings.author: Kenan Malik
counterReadings.note: Tracks how conflicts over blasphemy, race, and offense evolved after the Rushdie controversy.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
